title: Overriding and dynamic dispatch

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Overriding and dynamic dispatch; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. sources.locator: Learn Java; accessed 2026-07; Interfaces, Inheritance, Polymorphism, and composition guidance; atomic topic: Overriding and dynamic dispatch
sources.claim: An overridden instance method is selected at runtime from the receiver object's class, while overload resolution, static methods, private methods, and fields follow different compile-time rules.

sources.locator: Java Language Specification, Java SE 21; Java SE 21; JLS chapters 8-9 and §§15.12.2, 15.12.4: inheritance, interfaces, overload selection, and dispatch; atomic topic: Overriding and dynamic dispatch
sources.claim: An overridden instance method is selected at runtime from the receiver object's class, while overload resolution, static methods, private methods, and fields follow different compile-time rules.

canonicalExplanation.summary: An overridden instance method is selected at runtime from the receiver object's class, while overload resolution, static methods, private methods, and fields follow different compile-time rules.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A Shape reference holding a Circle invokes Circle.area at runtime, and @Override lets the compiler verify the intended signature. Your goal is to trace polymorphic calls and repair an accidental non-override. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Constructors and invariants; Class inheritance; Overloading and local scope. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: An overridden instance method is selected at runtime from the receiver object's class, while overload resolution, static methods, private methods, and fields follow different compile-time rules. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: A signature mismatch creates an overload instead of an override, covariant returns are allowed, constructors are not dispatched, and calling overridable methods during construction can observe incomplete state. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Every same-named member uses dynamic dispatch, including fields, static methods, overloads, and constructor calls.

includes: Predict runtime method selection and distinguish overriding from overloading and hiding.
Trace polymorphic calls and repair an accidental non-override.
excludes: A signature mismatch creates an overload instead of an override, covariant returns are allowed, constructors are not dispatched, and calling overridable methods during construction can observe incomplete state.

outcomes: Trace polymorphic calls and repair an accidental non-override.

examples.title: Overriding and dynamic dispatch: direct language case
examples.situation: A Shape reference holding a Circle invokes Circle.area at runtime, and @Override lets the compiler verify the intended signature.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A Shape reference holding a Circle invokes Circle.area at runtime, and @Override lets the compiler verify the intended signature.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Dynamic dispatch applies to overridden instance methods on the runtime receiver; use @Override and distinguish it from overload selection and member hiding.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Dynamic dispatch applies to overridden instance methods on the runtime receiver; use @Override and distinguish it from overload selection and member hiding.

examples.title: Overriding and dynamic dispatch: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A static method with the same signature is hidden rather than overridden, so the reference's compile-time type determines the selected declaration.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A static method with the same signature is hidden rather than overridden, so the reference's compile-time type determines the selected declaration.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: A signature mismatch creates an overload instead of an override, covariant returns are allowed, constructors are not dispatched, and calling overridable methods during construction can observe incomplete state.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Every same-named member uses dynamic dispatch, including fields, static methods, overloads, and constructor calls.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Trace polymorphic calls and repair an accidental non-override.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Every same-named member uses dynamic dispatch, including fields, static methods, overloads, and constructor calls.
misconceptions.correction: Dynamic dispatch applies to overridden instance methods on the runtime receiver; use @Override and distinguish it from overload selection and member hiding.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Every same-named member uses dynamic dispatch, including fields, static methods, overloads, and constructor calls.

analogy.example: a plug-and-socket system in which contracts describe compatible behavior while components hide their implementation can introduce Overriding and dynamic dispatch, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
limitations: overload resolution, method hiding, constructor order, and multiple interface defaults require Java-specific rules
